title: Understanding and Mitigating Numerical Sources of Nondeterminism in LLM Inference

reviews.summary: This paper investigates how numerical precision and system configuration introduce nondeterminism in LLM inference, even under “deterministic” greedy decoding. Through controlled experiments across GPU types, counts, and batch sizes, the authors show substantial accuracy and output-length variance—especially for reasoning models in BF16—trace the root cause to floating-point non-associativity, and quantify token-level instability. They propose LayerCast, a hybrid-precision inference pipeline that stores weights in 16-bit but performs all computations in FP32, achieving near-FP32 determinism with reduced memory versus full FP32.
strengths: The paper provides a careful, system-level attribution of nondeterminism to floating-point precision and non-associativity, connecting GPU kernel behavior and reduction order effects directly to LLM token selection.
Proposes LayerCast, a pragmatic inference pipeline that upcasts weights on-the-fly to execute all compute in FP32 while storing weights in 16-bit, aiming to balance determinism and memory efficiency.
Systematic sweep of 12 deployment configurations (2 GPU types × 2 GPU counts × 3 batch sizes) across multiple models (reasoning and non-reasoning) and benchmarks.
Multiple, complementary reproducibility metrics (Std@Acc, output length variance, divergence index, variance of top-1 probabilities) give a nuanced picture of instability mechanisms.
Both greedy and sampling settings are evaluated; the latter demonstrates that low precision inflates variance across system configurations even when averaging over runs.
Clear articulation of the floating-point formats, error sources, and the practical avenues (batching, TP/all-reduce, kernel choices) by which addition order changes on GPUs.
Intuitive figures and case studies showing how tiny logit differences flip top-1/top-2 tokens and cascade in long chains of thought.
Establishes that widely used BF16 evaluation pipelines can materially distort reported benchmark performance and length metrics, particularly for reasoning models.
Offers concrete guidance to the community (use FP32 for single-run greedy reporting; require more runs for BF16 sampling) and a usable implementation patch.
weaknesses: The “all computations in FP32” description for LayerCast is under-specified: it is unclear whether attention/KV cache storage and intermediate activations are also in FP32, and whether GEMMs use FP32 Tensor Core paths or fallback SGEMM, which has major performance implications.

The determinism claim with FP32 may not generalize to more varied kernels (e.g., FlashAttention settings, Split-K variants) or different vendor stacks; non-associativity still exists in FP32, albeit smaller.
No throughput/latency benchmarks for LayerCast relative to BF16/FP16/FP32 baselines; the practical cost of upcasting weights just-in-time and running FP32 GEMMs is not quantified.
Limited hardware coverage (A100, L40S); no H100/RTX4090/Hopper vs Ampere comparisons, nor CUDA/cuBLAS/cuDNN version sweeps, which are known sources of variance.
Batch-invariant kernels (e.g., FlexAttention/RMSNorm/MatMul) are discussed but not empirically compared as a baseline to isolate batch-size-related nondeterminism.
The role of tensor parallel size and all-reduce determinism is mentioned but not exhaustively ablated (e.g., TP=1 vs 2 vs 4 with fixed batch/driver/library).
Some terminology around “all computations in FP32” and “memory closer to 16-bit” is ambiguous without a precise memory breakdown (weights, activations, KV cache).
Minor typos and formatting artifacts (expected from PDF extraction) occasionally distract but do not block understanding.

There is growing practitioner work on determinism (e.g., deterministic cuBLASLt modes, stable top-k/sort kernels, compensated summation, Kahan/Pairwise summation) that could be acknowledged or tested.
The recent batch-invariant determinism work is referenced but not evaluated empirically; a direct comparison would strengthen claims.
reviews.questions:
1. LayerCast details: Which tensors are stored in 16-bit versus computed in FP32 (weights, activations, KV cache)? Is the KV cache stored in BF16/FP16 and upcast on read, or stored in FP32? Please provide a precise memory breakdown and clarify the “34% reduction vs full FP32.”
2. Performance: What are the end-to-end throughput/latency impacts of LayerCast compared to BF16/FP16 and full FP32 across sequence lengths, batch sizes, and GPU types? Are Tensor Cores used in FP32 compute or do you fall back to SGEMM?
3. Deterministic modes: Did you evaluate cuBLASLt deterministic algorithms, stable top-k/sort, or compensated (pairwise/Kahan) reductions? How do these compare to LayerCast and to BF16/FP16/FP32 baselines?
4. Kernel/back-end diversity: How does LayerCast perform with FlashAttention (v1/v2/v3) and Split-K GEMMs, and across CUDA/cuBLAS versions? Are results consistent on H100/RTX 4090?
5. Tensor parallelism: Can you report ablations over TP sizes (e.g., 1/2/4/8) with fixed batch size, including the contribution of all-reduce order and collective library settings to divergence?
6. Sampling pipelines: In random sampling, is the PRNG stream fully decoupled from floating-point compute order (e.g., separate generators per request)? Do FP32 vs BF16 runs consume identical random numbers for the same seeds and prompts?
7. Baselines: Can you include an empirical comparison to batch-invariant kernels for the batch-size axis and discuss how the two approaches might be combined (batch-invariance + FP32 compute)?
8. Generalizability: Have you tested instruction-tuned non-reasoning models at larger scales (e.g., 70B) and ultra-long contexts (>128k) to see if divergence behavior changes with context length and KV cache pressure?
reviews.assessment: This paper addresses an important and underappreciated source of nondeterminism in LLM inference: numerical precision interacting with GPU kernel execution order. The empirical evidence is compelling that BF16 can substantially distort greedy decoding outcomes and inflate variance in sampling-based evaluations, especially for reasoning models with small logit margins. The proposed LayerCast approach is practical and appears effective at recovering near-FP32 determinism while mitigating some memory overheads. The main areas to strengthen are (i) precise system details and performance characterization of LayerCast, (ii) broader hardware/kernel coverage, and (iii) empirical comparisons to existing determinism techniques (e.g., batch-invariant kernels, deterministic cuBLASLt). Despite these gaps, the work is timely, methodologically careful, and offers actionable guidance and code that could materially improve reproducibility practices. I recommend acceptance after addressing the questions above, particularly the performance and implementation clarifications for LayerCast.
